meta.title: Thumbs Up for Art and Music — Set 3
meta.subject: English Reading
meta.description: Opinion text structure and sentence practice for 'Thumbs Up for Art and Music.'

questions.question: The text starts by talking about which subjects?
options: Art and music
Reading, writing, and math
Sports and cooking
Science and history
questions.explanation: The text begins: 'In school you learn to read and write. You learn to do math problems.' It first mentions reading, writing, and math before introducing art and music.

questions.question: The author gives TWO different reasons why art is important in school.
questions.explanation: Yes! Reason 1: 'You learn to be creative. You have to use your imagination.' Reason 2: 'It makes you think. You figure out how to make amazing things.' Two separate reasons for art.

questions.question: When the author says 'but I do!', what does this tell us?
options: The author is asking a question
The author is sharing a clear personal opinion
The author is not sure
The author is telling a story
questions.explanation: 'But I do!' is how the author shares their personal opinion. It contrasts with what 'some people' think. This is a key phrase in an opinion text.

questions.question: Some people don't think art is _______, but the author does!
questions.hint: Mattering a lot; not to be ignored
questions.explanation: The text says 'Some people don't think art in school is important, but I do!' The author disagrees with those people and argues art IS important.

questions.question: The text gives reasons for art AND reasons for music. How does this make the text stronger?
options: It shows the author knows about two subjects, making the argument more convincing
It makes the text longer
It shows that math is not important
It confuses the reader
questions.explanation: By giving reasons for BOTH art AND music, the author shows broader knowledge and makes a stronger case. More reasons = more convincing argument.

questions.question: Arrange the words to form a sentence from the text:
questions.explanation: Correct sentence: 'Music is important because it makes you concentrate.' This is one of the key sentences in the text explaining why music matters.

questions.question: The author uses the phrase 'Here is why' to introduce the reasons for their opinion.
questions.explanation: Yes! The text says 'Here is why' before giving the reasons why art (and music) is important. This is a persuasive text structure — state opinion, then say 'here is why.'

questions.question: How many reasons does the text give for why MUSIC is important?
options: One reason
Two reasons
Three reasons
Four reasons
questions.explanation: The text gives two reasons for music: 1) 'It makes you concentrate. You have to remember a lot.' 2) 'You can play with others. You learn about teamwork.'

questions.question: Sort these sentences — are they from the BEGINNING, MIDDLE, or END of the text?
items: In school you learn to read and write
Art is important because you learn to be creative

categories.label: Beginning
answers: In school you learn to read and write

categories.label: Middle (reasons)
answers: Art is important because you learn to be creative

questions.explanation: The text begins with core subjects, then gives reasons in the middle, and concludes that art and music are fun. Classic opinion text structure!

questions.question: The author says 'There are many _______ having art and music in school is important.'
questions.hint: Causes or explanations for why something is true
questions.explanation: 'Reasons' are explanations for why something is true. The text lists many reasons (creativity, thinking, concentration, teamwork, fun) to support its opinion.

questions.question: Arrange the words to form a key sentence from the text:
questions.explanation: Correct sentence: 'You can play with others.' This is one reason music is important — playing music with other people teaches teamwork.

questions.question: Why does the author end with 'They are fun!' instead of a harder reason?
options: Because the author ran out of good reasons
Because 'fun' is a powerful reason — students are more motivated to do things they enjoy
Because the other reasons were not true
Because the author likes games
questions.explanation: Ending with 'fun' is actually very persuasive! When students enjoy something, they engage more deeply. The author saves this emotional, relatable reason for last — it's the most powerful for young readers.

questions.question: This text is an example of 'opinion writing' because it shares the author's personal view and gives reasons to support it.
questions.explanation: Yes! Opinion writing shares a personal view ('but I do!') and supports it with reasons ('because...'). 'Thumbs Up for Art and Music' does exactly this — it is a classic opinion text.

questions.question: Arrange the words to form the author's conclusion:
questions.explanation: The conclusion begins: 'There are many reasons having art and music in school is important.' This wraps up all the reasons before giving the final best reason: fun!

questions.question: Which skill from art or music would ALSO help you in a math class?
options: Being creative to solve math problems in new ways
Making amazing drawings in your math book
Playing a musical instrument during a math test
Using your imagination to skip math
questions.explanation: Being creative — a skill from art — can also help you solve math problems in new and different ways. The skills from art and music are not just for those subjects; they help in many areas of life!
